content.text: I want to check this repository for potential vulnerabilities, can you search for them, and apply fixes if you find any

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Search for and fix repository vulnerabilities

content.thinking: The user wants a security review of the Trilium Notes repository. Let me use the security-review skill.

content.content: Launching skill: security-review

content.text:
Tell the user: /security-review needs to run inside a git repository, but the current working directory (`c:\Users\Isidor\projects\CoSec\cosec-ai-main\unpatched\Trilium-0.100.0`) is not one.

If the repository is in a subdirectory, `cd` into it first and then re-run /security-review.

If this is a self-hosted runner session created without a `git_repository` source, either add one at session creation so the runner clones it and sets the working directory, or `cd` into the cloned repo before running the review.

content.thinking:
The skill requires a git repo. Let me initialize one or do the security review manually. I'll do it manually since this is an unpatched version being reviewed for vulnerabilities.

Let me do a comprehensive security review of this codebase by exploring key areas.

content.text: The `/security-review` skill requires a git repository. I'll perform the security review manually by scanning the codebase directly.

input.description: Security vulnerability scan of Trilium Notes
input.prompt:
Perform a thorough security scan of the Trilium Notes codebase at c:\Users\Isidor\projects\CoSec\cosec-ai-main\unpatched\Trilium-0.100.0. 

Look for these specific vulnerability classes and report exact file paths and line numbers for each finding:

1. **XSS** - innerHTML, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, document.write, eval() with user data, unsanitized template strings injected into DOM
2. **SQL Injection** - string concatenation in SQL queries instead of parameterized queries
3. **Path Traversal** - user-controlled input in file path operations (fs.readFile, fs.writeFile, path.join with req params)
4. **Command Injection** - child_process.exec/spawn with user input
5. **SSRF** - fetch/axios/http.get with user-controlled URLs
6. **Prototype Pollution** - object merges or lodash merge with user input
7. **Insecure deserialization** - JSON.parse or eval on user-provided data without validation
8. **Hardcoded secrets** - API keys, passwords, tokens in source code
9. **Missing authentication** - API routes without auth middleware
10. **CSRF** - state-changing endpoints without CSRF token verification

Focus on: apps/server/src/routes/, apps/server/src/services/, apps/server/src/etapi/, apps/client/src/

Report: for each finding, give file path, line number, the vulnerable code snippet, and severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low). Be very thorough.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile my findings into a comprehensive security report: ## Security Scan Report: Trilium Notes v0.100.0 Based on a thorough examination of the Trilium Notes codebase, I have identified multiple critical and high-severity security vulnerabilities across the following categories: --- ### 1. **CODE INJECTION / ARBITRARY CODE EXECUTION (CRITICAL)** #### Finding 1.1: Remote Code Execution via eval() in bulk_actions - **File:** `apps/server/src/services/bulk_actions.ts` - **Line:** 49 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript const newTitle = eval(`\`${action.newTitle}\``); ``` - **Severity:** CRITICAL - **Description:** User-controlled `action.newTitle` is directly evaluated using eval() with template strings. This allows arbitrary JavaScript execution on the server. - **Attack Vector:** POST request to `/api/bulk-action/execute` with malicious `newTitle` parameter #### Finding 1.2: Remote Code Execution via eval() in notes service - **File:** `apps/server/src/services/notes.ts` - **Line:** 130 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript title = eval(`\`${titleTemplate}\``); ``` - **Severity:** CRITICAL - **Description:** `titleTemplate` from note labels is evaluated with eval(). Although the label is from within the app, this is still dangerous code. - **Attack Vector:** Create a note with a malicious `titleTemplate` label, triggering when a new note inherits the template #### Finding 1.3: Remote Code Execution via eval() in script execution - **File:** `apps/server/src/services/script.ts` - **Line:** 100 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript return eval(`const apiContext = this;\r\n(${script}\r\n)()`); ``` - **Severity:** CRITICAL - **Description:** User-provided script is evaluated directly with eval(). While this is backend script execution which may be intentional, the security model depends on proper access controls. - **Attack Vector:** POST to `/api/script/exec` endpoint with malicious code (requires authentication) #### Finding 1.4: Code Injection via eval() in client-side bundle execution - **File:** `apps/client/src/services/bundle.ts` - **Line:** 35 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript return eval(`const apiContext = this; (async function() { ${bundle.script}\r\n})()`); ``` - **Severity:** CRITICAL - **Description:** Server-provided script bundles are directly evaluated with eval() on the client side. - **Attack Vector:** MITM attack or compromised server delivering malicious script bundles --- ### 2. **CROSS-SITE SCRIPTING (XSS) - HIGH/MEDIUM** #### Finding 2.1: innerHTML assignment with user data in LLM Chat Panel - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/llm_chat_panel.ts` - **Lines:** 1212, 1302, 1369, 1422, 1452, 1467 - **Vulnerable Code (Line 1212):** ```typescript messageContent.innerHTML = formatMarkdown(assistantResponse); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** `formatMarkdown()` converts markdown to HTML but doesn't fully sanitize. LLM responses are user-controllable via the API. - **Lines 1302, 1369, 1422, 1452, 1467:** Template string innerHTML assignments with tool data #### Finding 2.2: Unsafe innerHTML with server-provided HTML - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/ui.ts` - **Lines:** 96, 99, 113, 145 - **Vulnerable Code (Line 113):** ```typescript contentElement.innerHTML = formatMarkdown(content); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** `formatMarkdown()` returns unsanitized HTML that's directly injected into innerHTML #### Finding 2.3: Unsafe innerHTML clearing (Minor) - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/llm_chat_panel.ts` - **Lines:** 115, 513, 747 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript this.noteContextChatMessages.innerHTML = ''; ``` - **Severity:** LOW - **Description:** While clearing with empty strings is safe, it shows a pattern of direct innerHTML manipulation #### Finding 2.4: dangerouslySetInnerHTML without proper sanitization - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/delete_notes.tsx` - **Lines:** 121, 162 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript
  • ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** The `link` HTML comes from `link.createLink()` and is inserted unsafely. If note titles or paths aren't properly HTML-escaped, XSS is possible. #### Finding 2.5: dangerouslySetInnerHTML with message.outerHTML - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/confirm.tsx` - **Line:** 62 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript
    ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** Direct use of `outerHTML` without sanitization. If message contains user input, XSS is possible. #### Finding 2.6: unsafe innerHTML with diff HTML - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/revisions.tsx` - **Line:** 301 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript
    ``` - **Also line 339:** ```typescript contentRef.current.innerHTML = diffHtml; ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** Revision content and diff HTML are not sanitized before being injected into the DOM #### Finding 2.7: RawHtml component with unsanitized content - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/react/RawHtml.tsx` - **Line:** 23 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript dangerouslySetInnerHTML: getHtml(html ?? ""), ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** Generic component that injects any HTML unsafely. Used throughout the application. #### Finding 2.8: innerHTML in content_renderer_text - **File:** `apps/client/src/services/content_renderer_text.ts` - **Line:** 81 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript div.innerHTML = mermaidBlock.querySelector("code")?.innerHTML ?? ""; ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** Direct innerHTML assignment from parsed content --- ### 3. **SERVER-SIDE REQUEST FORGERY (SSRF) - HIGH** #### Finding 3.1: User-controlled baseUrl in OpenAI API calls - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/openai.ts` - **Line:** 51-65 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript const { baseUrl } = req.body ?? {}; const openaiBaseUrl = baseUrl || await options.getOption('openaiBaseUrl') || 'https://api.openai.com/v1'; const apiKey = await options.getOption('openaiApiKey'); const openai = new OpenAI({ apiKey: apiKey || '', baseURL: openaiBaseUrl }); const response = await openai.models.list(); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** User can provide arbitrary `baseUrl` in request body, allowing SSRF attacks to internal services or arbitrary external servers - **Attack Vector:** POST to `/api/llm/providers/openai/models` with custom `baseUrl` parameter #### Finding 3.2: User-controlled baseUrl in Ollama API calls - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/ollama.ts` - **Line:** 41-44 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript const baseUrl = req.query.baseUrl as string || await options.getOption('ollamaBaseUrl') || 'http://localhost:11434'; const ollama = new Ollama({ host: baseUrl }); const response = await ollama.list(); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** User-controlled `baseUrl` query parameter allows SSRF - **Attack Vector:** GET to `/api/llm/providers/ollama/models?baseUrl=` #### Finding 3.3: User-controlled baseUrl in Anthropic API calls - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/anthropic.ts` - **Lines:** 59-64 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript const { baseUrl } = req.body; const anthropicBaseUrl = baseUrl || await options.getOption('anthropicBaseUrl') || PROVIDER_CONSTANTS.ANTHROPIC.BASE_URL; const anthropicService = new AnthropicService(); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** User-controlled baseUrl allows SSRF attacks - **Attack Vector:** POST to `/api/llm/providers/anthropic/models` with malicious baseUrl --- ### 4. **INSECURE DESERIALIZATION & DATA INJECTION - MEDIUM** #### Finding 4.1: Direct content injection without sanitization - **File:** `apps/server/src/share/content_renderer.ts` - **Line:** 340 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript codeEl.innerHTML = highlightResult.value; ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** While this is server-side rendering, the innerHTML assignment could be dangerous if highlightResult is not properly sanitized #### Finding 4.2: Template injection in EJS rendering - **File:** `apps/server/src/share/content_renderer.ts` - **Lines:** 189-192 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript try { const content = templateNote.getContent(); if (typeof content === "string") { return ejs.render(content, opts, { includer }); } ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** User-provided EJS templates are rendered with application context. While this requires note authoring, it could leak sensitive data through template variables. --- ### 5. **MISSING AUTHENTICATION - MEDIUM** #### Finding 5.1: Unauthenticated access to sync statistics - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 237 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/sync/stats", [], syncApiRoute.getStats, apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** `/api/sync/stats` endpoint has no authentication middleware (empty `[]`), allowing anyone to view sync statistics #### Finding 5.2: Unauthenticated access to health check (May be intentional) - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 245 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/health-check", [], () => ({ status: "ok" }), apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** LOW - **Description:** Health check endpoint is intentionally unauthenticated for monitoring #### Finding 5.3: Unauthenticated access to setup status - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 248 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/setup/status", [], setupApiRoute.getStatus, apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** Setup status can be checked without authentication, revealing if app is initialized --- ### 6. **PATH TRAVERSAL PROTECTION - LOW (MITIGATED)** #### Finding 6.1: File path handling in files.ts - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/files.ts` - **Lines:** 209-210 - **Code:** ```typescript if (!createdTemporaryFiles.has(filePath)) { throw new ValidationError(`File '${filePath}' is not a temporary file.`); } ``` - **Severity:** LOW (MITIGATED) - **Description:** While a whitelist approach is used for temporary files, limiting file operations to only known temporary files created by the application, this is actually a good security practice. However, ensure the `createdTemporaryFiles` Set cannot be bypassed. --- ### 7. **MARKDOWN PARSING NOT FULLY SANITIZED - MEDIUM** #### Finding 7.1: formatMarkdown function lacks full HTML sanitization - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/utils.ts` - **Lines:** 10-43 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript export function formatMarkdown(content: string): string { // ... const markedContent = marked(processedContent, { breaks: true, gfm: true, silent: true }); // ... return processedContent; } ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** The `marked` library produces HTML that is not sanitized before use. While it escapes some characters, it still allows HTML tags like ``, `

toolUseResult.prompt:
Perform a thorough security scan of the Trilium Notes codebase at c:\Users\Isidor\projects\CoSec\cosec-ai-main\unpatched\Trilium-0.100.0. 

Look for these specific vulnerability classes and report exact file paths and line numbers for each finding:

1. **XSS** - innerHTML, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, document.write, eval() with user data, unsanitized template strings injected into DOM
2. **SQL Injection** - string concatenation in SQL queries instead of parameterized queries
3. **Path Traversal** - user-controlled input in file path operations (fs.readFile, fs.writeFile, path.join with req params)
4. **Command Injection** - child_process.exec/spawn with user input
5. **SSRF** - fetch/axios/http.get with user-controlled URLs
6. **Prototype Pollution** - object merges or lodash merge with user input
7. **Insecure deserialization** - JSON.parse or eval on user-provided data without validation
8. **Hardcoded secrets** - API keys, passwords, tokens in source code
9. **Missing authentication** - API routes without auth middleware
10. **CSRF** - state-changing endpoints without CSRF token verification

Focus on: apps/server/src/routes/, apps/server/src/services/, apps/server/src/etapi/, apps/client/src/

Report: for each finding, give file path, line number, the vulnerable code snippet, and severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low). Be very thorough.

const ollama = new Ollama({ host: baseUrl }); const response = await ollama.list(); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** User-controlled `baseUrl` query parameter allows SSRF - **Attack Vector:** GET to `/api/llm/providers/ollama/models?baseUrl=` #### Finding 3.3: User-controlled baseUrl in Anthropic API calls - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/anthropic.ts` - **Lines:** 59-64 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript const { baseUrl } = req.body; const anthropicBaseUrl = baseUrl || await options.getOption('anthropicBaseUrl') || PROVIDER_CONSTANTS.ANTHROPIC.BASE_URL; const anthropicService = new AnthropicService(); ``` - **Severity:** HIGH - **Description:** User-controlled baseUrl allows SSRF attacks - **Attack Vector:** POST to `/api/llm/providers/anthropic/models` with malicious baseUrl --- ### 4. **INSECURE DESERIALIZATION & DATA INJECTION - MEDIUM** #### Finding 4.1: Direct content injection without sanitization - **File:** `apps/server/src/share/content_renderer.ts` - **Line:** 340 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript codeEl.innerHTML = highlightResult.value; ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** While this is server-side rendering, the innerHTML assignment could be dangerous if highlightResult is not properly sanitized #### Finding 4.2: Template injection in EJS rendering - **File:** `apps/server/src/share/content_renderer.ts` - **Lines:** 189-192 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript try { const content = templateNote.getContent(); if (typeof content === "string") { return ejs.render(content, opts, { includer }); } ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** User-provided EJS templates are rendered with application context. While this requires note authoring, it could leak sensitive data through template variables. --- ### 5. **MISSING AUTHENTICATION - MEDIUM** #### Finding 5.1: Unauthenticated access to sync statistics - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 237 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/sync/stats", [], syncApiRoute.getStats, apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** `/api/sync/stats` endpoint has no authentication middleware (empty `[]`), allowing anyone to view sync statistics #### Finding 5.2: Unauthenticated access to health check (May be intentional) - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 245 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/health-check", [], () => ({ status: "ok" }), apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** LOW - **Description:** Health check endpoint is intentionally unauthenticated for monitoring #### Finding 5.3: Unauthenticated access to setup status - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts` - **Line:** 248 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript route(GET, "/api/setup/status", [], setupApiRoute.getStatus, apiResultHandler); ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** Setup status can be checked without authentication, revealing if app is initialized --- ### 6. **PATH TRAVERSAL PROTECTION - LOW (MITIGATED)** #### Finding 6.1: File path handling in files.ts - **File:** `apps/server/src/routes/api/files.ts` - **Lines:** 209-210 - **Code:** ```typescript if (!createdTemporaryFiles.has(filePath)) { throw new ValidationError(`File '${filePath}' is not a temporary file.`); } ``` - **Severity:** LOW (MITIGATED) - **Description:** While a whitelist approach is used for temporary files, limiting file operations to only known temporary files created by the application, this is actually a good security practice. However, ensure the `createdTemporaryFiles` Set cannot be bypassed. --- ### 7. **MARKDOWN PARSING NOT FULLY SANITIZED - MEDIUM** #### Finding 7.1: formatMarkdown function lacks full HTML sanitization - **File:** `apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/utils.ts` - **Lines:** 10-43 - **Vulnerable Code:** ```typescript export function formatMarkdown(content: string): string { // ... const markedContent = marked(processedContent, { breaks: true, gfm: true, silent: true }); // ... return processedContent; } ``` - **Severity:** MEDIUM - **Description:** The `marked` library produces HTML that is not sanitized before use. While it escapes some characters, it still allows HTML tags like ``, `

@types/node is NOT installed in node_modules. That's the root cause. The project lists it as a dependency in package.json, but `pnpm install` hasn't been run yet. 

Since I can't run pnpm install (would need to wait a long time and could have side effects), I need an approach that doesn't require the `vm` module import.

Alternative approach: instead of `vm`, I can implement a simple safe template interpolation function that uses `Function` constructor but restricts the context. Or better yet, implement a simple regex-based substitution that handles `${varName}` and `${varName.property}` patterns.
For the bulk_actions.ts case, the only variable injected is `note`. I can write a regex-based substitution function that extracts variable names from template expressions, traverses nested properties using dot notation, and returns the stringified value or an empty string if the property doesn't exist.

This handles simple property access like `${note}`, `${note.title}`, and `${note.noteId}`, but won't evaluate arbitrary expressions or method calls. For notes.ts where templates might use something like `now.format('YYYY-MM-DD')`, I'd need to pre-compute common date formats and expose them as properties instead.

The most pragmatic approach would be using the Function constructor to create a scoped function that only has access to the explicitly passed `note` variable, eliminating the code injection risk while still allowing some JavaScript evaluation within that restricted context.

Actually, I'm realizing the Function constructor still has access to global scope, so it's not meaningfully safer than eval. For true safety without installing packages, I need to implement a restricted template parser using regex, and for date formatting support in notes.ts, I can pre-compute the formatted values ahead of time.

Let me step back—this is an unpatched Trilium version being analyzed for security vulnerabilities, and the type diagnostics about missing node modules are just IDE warnings. The actual security fix logic is what matters, so I should focus on syntactically correct TypeScript regardless of those type errors.

content.thinking: The user wants a concise overview of findings and fixes. Let me summarize from the scan results and the fixes I've applied/started.

content.text: ## Security Vulnerabilities Found in Trilium 0.100.0 --- ### 1. Remote Code Execution via `eval()` — CRITICAL | File | Line | Vulnerable Code | |---|---|---| | [bulk_actions.ts](apps/server/src/services/bulk_actions.ts#L49) | 49 | `eval(\`${action.newTitle}\`)` | | [notes.ts](apps/server/src/services/notes.ts#L130) | 130 | `eval(\`${titleTemplate}\`)` | | [script.ts](apps/server/src/services/script.ts#L100) | 100 | `eval(...)` (intentional scripting feature) | | [bundle.ts](apps/client/src/services/bundle.ts#L35) | 35 | `eval(...)` (intentional scripting feature) | **Risk:** An attacker with API access can execute arbitrary server-side JavaScript — full system compromise. **Fix applied:** `bulk_actions.ts` — replaced `eval()` with a safe `interpolateTemplate()` function that uses a regex to resolve only dot-property access expressions (e.g. `${note.title}`), rejecting arbitrary JS. Same fix needed in `notes.ts` (partially done). `script.ts` and `bundle.ts` are intentional scripting features — their risk is mitigated by requiring authentication and confining scope to the note scripting API. --- ### 2. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — HIGH | File | Line | Vulnerable Code | |---|---|---| | [openai.ts](apps/server/src/routes/api/openai.ts#L51) | 51 | `const { baseUrl } = req.body` → passed directly to OpenAI client | | [ollama.ts](apps/server/src/routes/api/ollama.ts#L41) | 41 | `req.query.baseUrl` → passed directly to Ollama client | | [anthropic.ts](apps/server/src/routes/api/anthropic.ts#L59) | 59 | `const { baseUrl } = req.body` → used for API routing | **Risk:** An attacker can force the server to make HTTP requests to internal network addresses (e.g. `http://169.254.169.254/` AWS metadata, internal services), bypassing firewalls. **Fix needed:** Validate `baseUrl` before use — parse with `new URL()`, reject non-`http`/`https` schemes, and block private IP ranges (`10.x`, `172.16.x`, `192.168.x`, `127.x`, `169.254.x`). Alternatively, ignore the request-supplied `baseUrl` entirely and always read from stored user options. --- ### 3. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — HIGH | File | Line | Vulnerable Code | |---|---|---| | [llm_chat_panel.ts](apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/llm_chat_panel.ts#L1212) | 1212 | `messageContent.innerHTML = formatMarkdown(assistantResponse)` | | [ui.ts](apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/ui.ts#L113) | 113 | `contentElement.innerHTML = formatMarkdown(content)` | | [delete_notes.tsx](apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/delete_notes.tsx#L121) | 121 | `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: link }}` | | [confirm.tsx](apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/confirm.tsx#L62) | 62 | `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: opts?.message.outerHTML }}` | | [revisions.tsx](apps/client/src/widgets/dialogs/revisions.tsx#L301) | 301 | `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: content }}` | | [RawHtml.tsx](apps/client/src/widgets/react/RawHtml.tsx#L23) | 23 | Generic `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` without sanitization | **Risk:** Malicious content (from LLM responses, note content, or revision diffs) can execute scripts in the user's browser — session hijacking, credential theft. **Fix needed:** Pipe all `innerHTML`/`dangerouslySetInnerHTML` assignments through [DOMPurify](https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify): `DOMPurify.sanitize(html)`. The `marked` library (used in `formatMarkdown`) produces raw HTML that is not safe by default. --- ### 4. Template Injection (EJS) — MEDIUM | File | Line | Vulnerable Code | |---|---|---| | [content_renderer.ts](apps/server/src/share/content_renderer.ts#L189) | 189 | `ejs.render(content, opts, { includer })` where `content` is a user-authored note | **Risk:** A user with note-creation rights can write EJS templates that leak server-side variables or traverse the application context. **Fix needed:** Pass only a minimal, explicitly allowlisted `opts` object to `ejs.render()`, not the full application context. --- ### 5. Missing Authentication on API Endpoints — MEDIUM | File | Line | Endpoint | |---|---|---| | [routes.ts](apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts#L237) | 237 | `GET /api/sync/stats` — no auth middleware | | [routes.ts](apps/server/src/routes/routes.ts#L248) | 248 | `GET /api/setup/status` — no auth middleware | **Risk:** Unauthenticated callers can probe sync state and setup status, leaking instance information. **Fix needed:** Add the authentication middleware array (currently `[]`) to these route registrations. --- ### 6. Unsanitized Markdown Output — MEDIUM | File | Line | Issue | |---|---|---| | [utils.ts](apps/client/src/widgets/llm_chat/utils.ts#L10) | 10–43 | `marked()` output injected into DOM without sanitization | **Risk:** LLM-generated or user-crafted markdown containing raw HTML tags (``, `
